title: Reading Comprehension
objective: Students will explore reading comprehension concepts including Inferring Characters' Feelings and Motives, Main Ideas & Note-Taking, Forms of Poetry and Performance and more.

steps.content: Have you ever thought about Inferring Characters' Feelings and Motives? Let's find out together!
Draw inferences from independently-read texts, such as inferring characters' feelings, thoughts and motives from their actions, and justifying inferences with evidence from the text
Let's practice together! Infer a character's feelings or motives from their actions and dialogue (e.g. 'She slammed the door — how is she feeling?')
After you reads a chapter of a novel, can they explain how a character was feeling and why — pointing to something the character said or did as their evidence?

Great job! Today we learned about Inferring Characters' Feelings and Motives. Infer a character's feelings or motives from their actions and dialogue (e.g. 'She slammed the door — how is she feeling?') Justify an inference by quoting or pointing to specific evidence in the text
Today we're going to learn about Main Ideas & Note-Taking. Get ready for an adventure!
Identify main ideas drawn from more than one paragraph and summarise them; retrieve and record information from non-fiction texts using notes, tables or other methods
Time to try this out: Read a multi-paragraph non-fiction text and state the main idea of each paragraph in one sentence
When you reads a long non-fiction article or chapter, can they pull out the most important points and write them as a short summary — rather than just copying chunks of text?

Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Main Ideas & Note-Taking. Read a multi-paragraph non-fiction text and state the main idea of each paragraph in one sentence Summarise a text of 3+ paragraphs in 2-3 sentences capturing the key points
Have you ever thought about Forms of Poetry and Performance? Let's find out together!

Recognise different forms of poetry (free verse, narrative poetry, haiku) and discuss their features; prepare poems and play scripts to read aloud and perform with understanding through intonation, tone, volume and action

Let me show you something cool. Name at least two different forms of poetry and describe a feature of each (e.g., 'narrative poetry tells a story', 'free verse does not have a regular rhyme or rhythm')
When you reads a poem aloud, do they change their voice to match the mood — reading excitedly for a lively poem or quietly for a sad one — rather than reading it flat?

Great job! Today we learned about Forms of Poetry and Performance. Name at least two different forms of poetry and describe a feature of each (e.g., 'narrative poetry tells a story', 'free verse does not have a regular rhyme or rhythm') Recognise the form of a given poem and explain how you identified it (e.g., 'This is a narrative poem because it has characters and a plot')
Welcome to an exciting lesson about Themes and messages!

Identify recurring themes (good vs evil, friendship, bravery) and conventions (once upon a time, moral at the end, hero's journey) across a wide range of books including fairy stories, myths, legends and traditional tales
Let's practice together! Identify a common theme across two different stories (e.g., 'Both "Jack and the Beanstalk" and "The Three Billy Goats Gruff" are about bravery overcoming a threat')
After reading a few different fairy tales or myths, can you spot patterns — like "the hero always faces three challenges" or "good always wins in the end" — across the different stories?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Themes and messages: Identify a common theme across two different stories (e.g., 'Both "Jack and the Beanstalk" and "The Three Billy Goats Gruff" are about bravery overcoming a threat') Name literary conventions found in fairy tales and myths (e.g., 'once upon a time', magical numbers like three, a quest or journey, good triumphing over evil)
Let's explore Story Lessons and Morals! Have you ever wondered about this?
Determine the central message, lesson, or moral of a story, fable, or folktale, explaining what the story teaches the reader and supporting the interpretation with key details from the text
Let's practice together! State the lesson or moral of a fable in one sentence (e.g. 'The Tortoise and the Hare teaches that slow and steady wins the race')
After reading a fable like "The Tortoise and the Hare", can you tell you the lesson the story is teaching — and point to a moment in the story that shows that lesson?

Great job! Today we learned about Story Lessons and Morals. State the lesson or moral of a fable in one sentence (e.g. 'The Tortoise and the Hare teaches that slow and steady wins the race') Explain what a story's central message is using at least two details from the text as support
Let's discover the amazing world of Text Features & Presentation!
Identify how language choices, text structure and presentational features (illustrations, diagrams, bold print, layout) contribute to the overall meaning and effect of a text
Let me show you something cool. Explain how an author's word choices create a particular effect (e.g., 'The author uses "crept" instead of "walked" to make it feel sneaky and tense')
When you reads a page with headings, diagrams, or bold words, can they explain how those features help — like "the diagram shows the water cycle because the words alone are hard to picture"?

Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Text Features & Presentation. Explain how an author's word choices create a particular effect (e.g., 'The author uses "crept" instead of "walked" to make it feel sneaky and tense') Identify how a text's structure helps the reader (e.g., 'The headings help you find information quickly', 'The story builds suspense before the ending')
